questions.question:
Choose the best matching between the programming styles in Group 1 and their characteristics in Group 2.

Group 1:
P. Functional
Q. Logic
R. Object-oriented
S. Imperative

Group 2:
1. Common-based, procedural
2. Inquisitive, abstract data types
3. Side-effect free, declarative, expression evaluations
4. Declarative, clausal representation, theorem proving

A. P-2  Q-3  R-4  S-1
B. P-4  Q-3  R-2  S-1
C. P-3  Q-4  R-1  S-2
D. P-3  Q-4  R-2  S-1
questions.to_find: Best matching of programming paradigms (Group 1) to their characteristics (Group 2)

understand.plain: The question asks you to match four programming paradigms (Functional, Logic, Object-oriented, Imperative) with their defining characteristics. Each paradigm has a distinct philosophy: Functional programming avoids side effects and relies on expression evaluation; Logic programming uses clausal (rule-based) representation and theorem proving; Object-oriented programming uses abstract data types; Imperative programming is statement/command-based and procedural.

keywords.explain: A paradigm that treats computation as the evaluation of mathematical functions, avoiding changing state and mutable data (side-effect free).
keywords.example: Haskell, Lisp: f(x) = x*x is a pure function with no side effects.

keywords.explain: A paradigm based on formal logic where programs are sets of sentences in logical form (clauses) and execution is theorem proving.
A paradigm that organises code into objects encapsulating data (abstract data types) and behaviour (methods).
keywords.example: Java: class Stack { private int[] data; void push(int x){...} }

keywords.explain: A paradigm where the programmer gives explicit step-by-step commands (statements) to change program state; it is procedural and common-based.
keywords.example: C: int sum=0; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) sum+=a[i];

understand.visual_alt: Table with two groups: Group 1 has Functional, Logic, Object-oriented, Imperative; Group 2 has four characteristics that must be matched one-to-one.

given.aim: Match each programming paradigm in Group 1 to its correct characteristic in Group 2.

terms.meaning: Computation through expression evaluation, no side effects, declarative.
terms.example: Pure function: f(n) = n! expressed recursively.
terms.connects: Matches characteristic 3: Side-effect free, declarative, expression evaluations.

terms.meaning: Programs written as logical clauses; execution is a proof search (theorem proving).
terms.example: Prolog rules and facts form clauses.
terms.connects: Matches characteristic 4: Declarative, clausal representation, theorem proving.

terms.meaning: Organises code around objects that are instances of abstract data types.
terms.connects: Matches characteristic 2: Inquisitive, abstract data types.

terms.meaning: Sequential commands that change state; procedural, common-based style.
terms.example: C, Pascal programs with loops and assignments.

given.plan: Recall the defining property of each paradigm: Functional = side-effect free expression evaluation (3); Logic = clausal/theorem proving (4); Object-oriented = abstract data types (2); Imperative = procedural commands (1). Check which answer option gives P-3, Q-4, R-2, S-1.

steps.title: Match Functional (P) to its characteristic
steps.apply: Functional programming is defined by: no side effects, computation via expression evaluation, declarative style. This matches Group 2 item 3: 'Side-effect free, declarative, expression evaluations'. So P -> 3.
steps.note: Functional languages like Haskell/ML are explicitly designed to be side-effect free.

steps.title: Match Logic (Q) to its characteristic
steps.apply: Logic programming (Prolog) uses clauses (Horn clauses) and the runtime performs theorem proving (resolution). This matches Group 2 item 4: 'Declarative, clausal representation, theorem proving'. So Q -> 4.
steps.note: Prolog programs are sets of facts and rules; the interpreter searches for proofs.

steps.title: Match Object-oriented (R) and Imperative (S)
steps.apply: Object-oriented programming encapsulates data into abstract data types (objects/classes), matching item 2: 'Inquisitive, abstract data types'. So R -> 2. Imperative programming is the traditional procedural style (C, Pascal) matching item 1: 'Common-based, procedural'. So S -> 1.
steps.note: The remaining mapping is forced once P and Q are assigned.

steps.title: Select the matching answer option
steps.apply: P-3, Q-4, R-2, S-1 corresponds to option D.
steps.note: Check each option: A gives P-2 (wrong), B gives P-4 (wrong), C gives R-1 (wrong). Only D is correct.

questions.formula_note: Classification of programming paradigms: Functional (side-effect free, expression evaluation), Logic (clausal/theorem-proving), Object-oriented (abstract data types), Imperative (procedural commands).

questions.question:
Consider the following recursive definition of fib:

fib(n) := if n = 0 then 1
           else if n = 1 then 1
           else fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)

The number of times fib is called (including the first call) for evaluation of fib(7) is ___________.
questions.to_find: Total number of times fib() is called (including the first call) when computing fib(7)

understand.plain: This recursive Fibonacci function calls itself twice for every n > 1. Each call spawns two more calls, leading to a binary call tree. We need to count every node in this call tree (each node is one function call) when computing fib(7). The total grows exponentially.

keywords.explain: A definition where fib(n) is computed by adding fib(n-1) and fib(n-2), with base cases fib(0)=1 and fib(1)=1.
keywords.example: fib(3) = fib(2)+fib(1) = (fib(1)+fib(0))+1 = 3 calls just for this subtree.

keywords.explain: The total number of recursive calls made when evaluating fib(n), satisfying T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + 1 with T(0)=T(1)=1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='200' viewBox='0 0 500 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='220' y='10' width='60' height='30' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>fib(7)</text><line x1='250' y1='40' x2='160' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='250' y1='40' x2='340' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='130' y='70' width='60' height='28' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='89' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>fib(6)</text><rect x='310' y='70' width='60' height='28' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='340' y='89' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>fib(5)</text><text x='250' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>... each node spawns two children until fib(0) or fib(1) ...</text><text x='250' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>T(7) = 41 total calls</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Binary call tree rooted at fib(7); each internal node fib(n) has children fib(n-1) and fib(n-2). Total nodes (calls) = 41.

given.aim: Count total function calls (including initial call) to evaluate fib(7) using the given naive recursive definition.

terms.meaning: Base: fib(0)=fib(1)=1; Recursive: fib(n) = fib(n-1)+fib(n-2) for n>1.
terms.connects: Each recursive call spawns exactly two more calls, leading to the recurrence for T(n).

terms.meaning: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + 1, with T(0)=1 and T(1)=1 (base calls each count as 1).
terms.connects: Solving this up to T(7) gives the answer.

terms.meaning: The initial call fib(7) itself counts as 1 in T(7).
terms.example: For fib(0), T(0)=1 (just that one call).

given.plan: Set up the recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + 1 with T(0)=T(1)=1. Compute T(2) through T(7) iteratively.

steps.title: Establish the recurrence for call count
steps.apply: When fib(n) is called (n>1), it makes one call to fib(n-1) which costs T(n-1) calls, one call to fib(n-2) which costs T(n-2) calls, plus 1 for the current call itself. Base cases: fib(0) and fib(1) each make exactly 1 call (no recursion).
steps.note: This recurrence looks like Fibonacci itself but shifted by the +1 term.

steps.title: Compute T(0) through T(7) step by step
steps.note: T(n) = 2*fib(n) - 1 where fib is the standard Fibonacci. fib(7)=21, so T(7)=2*21-1=41. Both methods agree.

questions.formula_note: Recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + 1 for call count in naive recursive Fibonacci; closed form T(n) = 2*fib(n) - 1.

questions.to_find: Maximum value of n for which the stack does not overflow when running fib(n) with a 64-byte stack

understand.plain: Each call to fib(n) pushes a stack frame containing the return address (2 bytes) and the parameter n (2 bytes), totalling 4 bytes per frame. The recursive calls go deepest along the left branch: fib(n) -> fib(n-1) -> fib(n-2) -> ... -> fib(0). The maximum stack depth for fib(n) is n+1 frames. With 64 bytes available, we can fit at most 16 frames, meaning n can be at most 15.

keywords.explain: The block of memory pushed onto the call stack for each function call, containing the return address and local parameters.
keywords.example: Calling fib(5) pushes: [return address (2B), parameter 5 (2B)] = 4 bytes.

keywords.explain: The deepest chain of nested calls before reaching a base case. For fib(n), the depth is n+1 (fib(n), fib(n-1), ..., fib(0)).
keywords.example: fib(3): depth = 4 frames: fib(3)->fib(2)->fib(1) or fib(3)->fib(2)->fib(0).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='220' viewBox='0 0 420 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='180' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>fib(n) frame: 4B</text><rect x='10' y='45' width='180' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>fib(n-1) frame: 4B</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='180' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>fib(n-2) frame: 4B</text><text x='100' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>... (n+1 frames total)</text><rect x='10' y='140' width='180' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>fib(0) frame: 4B</text><text x='230' y='50' class='ink lbl'>Stack: 64 B</text><text x='230' y='75' class='ink sub'>Max frames = 64/4 = 16</text><text x='230' y='100' class='ink sub'>depth = n+1 <= 16</text><text x='230' y='125' class='ink sub'>n <= 15</text><text x='230' y='155' class='ok lbl'>Max n = 15</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Stack growing downward showing frames fib(n), fib(n-1), ..., fib(0) each 4 bytes; 16 frames fit in 64 bytes so max n=15.

given.aim: Find the maximum n such that evaluating fib(n) does not cause a stack overflow on a 64-byte stack.

terms.example: Each frame occupies 4 bytes, so up to 16 frames can be pushed.
terms.connects: Dividing 64 by frame size gives maximum number of simultaneous frames.

terms.meaning: Return address (2 bytes) + parameter n (2 bytes) = 4 bytes per call.
terms.connects: Determines how many calls can be active at once.

terms.meaning: For fib(n), deepest call path goes fib(n)->fib(n-1)->...->fib(1) or fib(0), which is n+1 frames deep.
terms.connects: n+1 frames must fit in 64 bytes, so n+1 <= 16, giving n <= 15.

given.plan: Compute frame size = 4 bytes. Maximum frames = 64/4 = 16. The deepest recursion for fib(n) has depth n+1. Set n+1 <= 16 to get n <= 15.

steps.title: Compute the size of one stack frame
steps.formula_raw: frame size = size(return address) + size(parameter) = 2 + 2 = 4 bytes
steps.apply: Each call to fib pushes: return address = 2 bytes, integer parameter n = 2 bytes. Total per frame = 4 bytes.
steps.note: The problem states only return address and parameter are on the stack, nothing else.

steps.title: Find maximum number of frames that fit
steps.apply: With 64 bytes total and 4 bytes per frame, the stack can hold at most 16 frames simultaneously.
steps.note: Stack overflow occurs when the 17th frame would be needed.

steps.title: Relate call depth to n
steps.formula_raw: max depth for fib(n) = n + 1 frames
steps.apply: The deepest call chain for fib(n) is: fib(n) -> fib(n-1) -> fib(n-2) -> ... -> fib(1) -> fib(0). This is n+1 nested calls (n, n-1, n-2, ..., 1, 0 = n+1 values). So maximum depth = n+1.
steps.note: The left-most branch always goes all the way down to fib(0), giving depth n+1.

steps.title: Solve for maximum n
steps.apply: For the stack not to overflow: n + 1 <= 16, so n <= 15. The maximum value of n is 15.
steps.note: At n=15, depth = 16 frames = exactly 64 bytes (no overflow). At n=16, depth = 17 frames = 68 bytes (overflow).

questions.formula_note: Stack frame size = return address + parameter bytes; maximum recursion depth for fib(n) = n+1; stack constraint: (n+1)*frame_size <= stack_size.

questions.answer:
A. (1): f[n] != 0   (2): f[n]   (and add after return: f[n] = fib(n-1)+fib(n-2) before returning)
B. O(n)
questions.to_find: Fill in blanks to implement memoized Fibonacci and state the resulting time complexity

understand.plain: The naive recursive Fibonacci recomputes the same subproblems exponentially many times. To fix this, we use memoization: store the result of fib(k) in f[k] the first time it is computed. On subsequent calls with the same k, check if f[k] is already computed (non-zero) and return it directly without recursing again. This makes the time complexity linear.

keywords.explain: An optimization technique where previously computed results are stored in a cache (here the array f[]) and reused on repeat calls, avoiding redundant computation.
keywords.example: After computing fib(5)=8, store f[5]=8. Next call to fib(5) returns 8 immediately.

keywords.explain: Each distinct value fib(0), fib(1), ..., fib(n) is computed exactly once. After computing, the result is cached. So total work is O(n).
keywords.example: Without memoization fib(30) makes millions of calls; with memoization only 31 unique computations.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='180' viewBox='0 0 460 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='25' class='lbl ink'>Memo array f[]:</text><rect x='10' y='35' width='50' height='35' rx='4' class='ok'/><text x='35' y='57' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:white'>f[0]=1</text><rect x='65' y='35' width='50' height='35' rx='4' class='ok'/><text x='90' y='57' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:white'>f[1]=1</text><rect x='120' y='35' width='50' height='35' rx='4' class='ok'/><text x='145' y='57' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:white'>f[2]=2</text><rect x='175' y='35' width='50' height='35' rx='4' class='ok'/><text x='200' y='57' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:white'>f[3]=3</text><rect x='230' y='35' width='50' height='35' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='255' y='57' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>f[4]=0</text><text x='290' y='57' class='mut sub'>(not yet computed)</text><text x='10' y='110' class='ink sub'>Blank (1): f[n] != 0   (check if already computed)</text><text x='10' y='135' class='ink sub'>Blank (2): f[n]        (return cached value)</text><text x='10' y='160' class='ink sub'>Also store: f[n] = fib(n-1)+fib(n-2) before returning in last line</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array f[] showing cached values; blanks filled with f[n]!=0 to check cache and f[n] to return cached result.

given.aim: Complete the memoized Fibonacci code and determine its time complexity.

terms.meaning: Acts as the memoization cache; f[k]=0 means fib(k) has not been computed yet; f[k]!=0 means fib(k) was already computed and cached.
terms.example: Initially all zeros; after fib(3) is computed, f[3]=3.
terms.connects: Blank (1) checks f[n]!=0 to detect a cached result; blank (2) returns f[n].

terms.meaning: f[n] != 0 means the Fibonacci value for n has already been stored in the array.
terms.connects: Short-circuits the recursion for already-computed values, making each value computed only once.

terms.meaning: f[n] holds the previously computed fib(n) value that we return directly.
terms.connects: Together with storing f[n] = fib(n-1)+fib(n-2) after the recursive calls, ensures completeness.

terms.meaning: With memoization, each of the n+1 subproblems (fib(0) to fib(n)) is solved exactly once. Total work = O(n).
terms.connects: After filling the blanks, the function behaves like a linear scan through values 0 to n.

given.plan: For blank (1): check if f[n] is non-zero (i.e., already computed). For blank (2): return f[n]. Also, just before the last return, store f[n] = fib(n-1)+fib(n-2). Time complexity: O(n) since each value computed once.

steps.title: Identify what blanks (1) and (2) should do
steps.apply: The pattern for memoization is: before recursing, check if the answer is already in the cache. If f[n] != 0, the value was already computed and stored, so return it directly. Blank (1) = 'f[n] != 0', Blank (2) = 'f[n]'.
steps.note: Since Fibonacci values are always positive (>=1), f[n]=0 safely means 'not yet computed'.

steps.title: Complete the full memoized function
steps.note: The storage step f[n] = fib(n-1)+fib(n-2) is essential; without it, blank (1) would never be true.

steps.title: Determine time complexity
steps.apply: With memoization, fib(k) is computed (recursively) only once for each k in {0, 1, ..., n}. Once f[k] is set, all subsequent calls fib(k) return in O(1). There are n+1 unique subproblems, each solved in O(1) work (ignoring sub-calls which are all cache hits). Total time = O(n).
steps.note: This is a dramatic improvement from the exponential O(2^n) of naive recursion, turning it into a dynamic programming solution.

solution.result:
A. Blank (1): f[n] != 0   Blank (2): f[n]   (store f[n] = fib(n-1)+fib(n-2) before returning)
B. Time complexity: O(n)

questions.formula_note: Memoization (top-down dynamic programming) reduces Fibonacci time complexity from O(2^n) to O(n) by caching and reusing subproblem results.
